title: Combinatorial affine sieve
speaker: Alireza Salehi Golsefidy
abstract: In this talk the general setting of affine sieve will be presented. Next I will explain the Bourgain-Gamburd-Sarnak method on proving affine sieve in the presence of certain spectral gap. Finally I will say how one can prove the fundamental theorem of affine sieve by getting such a result in the absence of spectral gap.
tags: Workshop on Thin Groups and Super Approximation

proof_stages.label: Classical sieve motivation and almost-prime relaxation
proof_stages.summary: The lecture begins with one-parameter prime-value problems, explaining local obstructions for linear polynomials, the usefulness of almost primes in classical sieve questions, and the failure of sieve methods on very sparse sequences.

proof_stages.label: Zariski-density formulation of affine sieve
proof_stages.summary: Following Bourgain-Gamburd-Sarnak, the talk replaces 'infinitely many' by Zariski density in multi-parameter settings and formulates affine sieve for finitely generated matrix groups, regular functions, and localized bounded-prime-factor values.

proof_stages.label: Character obstructions and the main no-character theorem
proof_stages.summary: Multiplicative cyclic groups and tori indicate that characters allow bad functions to be pulled back, motivating the hypothesis that the connected Zariski closure have no characters; under this hypothesis the main theorem asserts Zariski density of almost-prime values for every non-zero-divisor regular function.

proof_stages.label: Bourgain-Gamburd-Sarnak spectral-gap input
proof_stages.summary: The previously known BGS theorem is recalled as the sieve result available when the function is primitive and absolutely irreducible and the congruence actions have a uniform spectral gap, especially for squarefree moduli in the SL2 case.

proof_stages.label: Perfect-core and unipotent-quotient reduction
proof_stages.summary: After reducing to connected Zariski closure, repeated derived subgroups yield a connected perfect normal subgroup H, while the quotient U is unipotent and admits a rational section, allowing G to be viewed as a variety product H x U with dense projected subgroups.

proof_stages.label: Regular-function decomposition into base and fiber data
proof_stages.summary: Using the product structure G ≅ H x U, the regular function f is written as a finite sum of base functions times fiber functions, a gcd p0 is extracted on the unipotent base, and the value of f on a fiber becomes p0 times a shifted linear combination of the fiber functions.

proof_stages.label: Unipotent combinatorial sieve for good base points
proof_stages.summary: A stronger unipotent sieve theorem supplies a Zariski dense set of base points for which p0 has boundedly many localized prime factors and the remaining base coefficients p1,...,pm take mutually coprime localized values.

proof_stages.label: Uniform perfect-fiber sieve and gluing
proof_stages.summary: A uniform spectral-gap sieve theorem for connected perfect groups handles all primitive linear combinations and shifts of finitely many fiber functions with common r and q0, and combining it with the unipotent base theorem proves the desired affine-sieve density result.

examples.name: Mersenne primes as a sparse multiplicative orbit
examples.note: The sequence 2^n-1 and its affine-sieve reinterpretation are used to show why sparse multiplicative groups and pulled-back bad functions force a no-character condition.
